summary.text:
Events, Emitters and Disposables are very common concepts that many codebases will contain, however we need
to export them for dockview framework packages to use.
To be a good citizen these are exported with a
prefix to prevent accidental use by others.

Opt this edge group in/out of auto-hide (pinnable tool-window) behaviour,
overriding the global

option. Requires the
auto-hide module to have any effect. Leave unset to inherit the global.

When true, this edge group tears itself down to zero footprint once
emptied (instead of collapsing to a strip) — the behaviour used by
drag-revealed edges.

A framework component to render as the menu item.
The component type is opaque to core; the framework adapter renders it.

Use this when you want to render custom content without a framework component.

Whether this group is locked against drop interactions.
-

: panels cannot be dropped into the group (center / tabs),
  but the group can still be split from its edges.
-
: all drop zones are disabled for this group.

Fires when the active panel *within this group* changes. Scoped to the
group, in contrast to the component-level

(which tracks the active panel across
the whole dockview). Both carry an

reporting
whether the change came from a user gesture or an API call.

Fired when an edge group's collapsed state changes.
Never fires for non-edge groups.

overlay shown/hidden while the group stays logically collapsed). Never
fires for non-edge groups or without the auto-hide module.

Built-in screen-reader announcements of layout changes (a visually-hidden

region narrating panel open/close etc.). On by default —
set to

to disable, e.g. when the host app provides its own
announcement system. Honoured live via

Route announcements to your own screen-reader infrastructure instead of
the built-in

regions (e.g. an app-wide live region). When
set, dockview hands you each

and writes nothing
to its own regions.

VS Code-style "auto hide" for edge groups: render clickable activators in
a collapsed edge group's strip so it can be pinned back. Off by default →
today's baseline (an empty collapsed strip) is unchanged.

A per-edge set:

applies to all four edges, or name edges
individually (

). An edge group at an edge
not in the set stays a static collapsing sidebar. A per-group

override wins over this default. Peek
animation is tuned globally via

Factory to create the custom ghost element shown while dragging a
group of panels (the small floating chip that follows the cursor).

If not provided, a default ghost rendering

is used. Supplying a factory replaces the entire default ghost,
enabling i18n / custom visuals.

Factory to create custom tab group chip renderers.
If not provided, the default chip renderer is used.
Disable the auto-resizing which is controlled through a

Show an aim-at-a-cell "compass" drop guide over a group while dragging,
instead of resolving the drop by which quadrant the cursor is in. Default
off ⇒ the cursor-quadrant behaviour, unchanged. Provided by the Drop Guide
module. Pass an object to restrict which inner cells appear (

) or to
hide the outer whole-layout-edge cells (

Selects which drag-and-drop implementation is active.

-

(default): HTML5 drag-and-drop drives mouse drags; pointer
  events drive touch and pen drags. Matches the historical behaviour.
-

: pointer events drive every input type. Useful in
  environments where HTML5 drag-and-drop is unreliable (some Linux
  browsers, certain Safari versions, embedded webviews). Cross-window
  HTML5 drag and the HTML5 native drag image are not available in this
  mode.
-
: HTML5 drag-and-drop only — disables touch / pen drag.

Let panels dragged to a layout edge create/fill an **edge group** that is
invisible when empty (VS Code-style "drag a panel to the far edge → new
sidebar"). Created on drop; torn down to zero footprint when its last
panel leaves.

A per-edge set:

enables dock-to-edge on all four edges, or name
edges individually. Requires both the
modules; a no-op if either is absent. Distinct from

, which only shapes the outer drop overlay (and still splits
the grid). Off by default.

Shape the drop overlay shown over a group's drop targets — the tab
strip (

to override that target's default overlay (size, activation threshold,
small-element boundaries), or

is provided where known (tab / header_space). The outer-layout
edge overlay is shaped by
Override how a pointer location maps to a drop

for no drop) on the 5-way group/layout drop targets — the group content
and the whole-layout edges — replacing the built-in cursor-quadrant logic.
Tab/header reorder targets are unaffected. Unset ⇒ the default quadrant
behaviour, unchanged. Read live, so it can be swapped via

Selects which element moves a floating group when dragged.

-

(default): a dedicated, blank drag-handle bar is rendered
  above the group's tab bar. Dragging it moves the floating window;
  shift+drag (mouse) / long-press (touch) redocks into the grid. Style
  it with the

: the legacy behaviour — the empty space in the tab bar
  (the "void container") doubles as the move handle. No dedicated bar
  is rendered.

Localise or override the built-in announcement strings (the default
messages are English). Return a string to use it,

to keep the default. This is
how non-English apps translate announcements — core ships no message
catalog, only the default strings + this hook.

Return the items to display in the tab context menu on right-click.

Use built-in string shortcuts or provide a

object for custom items. The available shortcuts are:
-

— close every other panel in the group
-

— close every panel in the group
-

— close the panels before / after this
  one in the tab strip
-

— move the panel into a floating window (disabled when
  already floating)
-

— move the panel into a new browser window (disabled when
  already popped out)
-

— toggle the panel's pinned state (PinnedTabs module)
-

— a divider line

If omitted, no context menu is shown.
Return an empty array to suppress the menu for specific cases.

Return the items to display in the tab group chip context menu on right-click.

Use built-in string shortcuts or provide a

object for custom items. The available shortcuts are:
-

— renders an inline text input to rename the tab group
-

— renders a grid of color swatches for the tab group
-

— collapse the tab group (renders as *Expand* when the
  group is already collapsed)
-

— close every panel belonging to the tab group
-

— a divider line

If omitted, no context menu is shown on chip right-click.
Return an empty array to suppress the menu for specific cases.
Operate the dock with the keyboard.

enables the default bindings;
pass an object to override individual ones via

. Off by default
(opt-in while the feature matures). Enables:

- **Switch tab** within the focused group —

+arrow keys (spatial: focus the group in that direction).

arms a two-phase

shown in the context menu picker.

Adjust a floating group's position while it is being dragged. Runs on
each pointer-move frame with the proposed top-left (before the container
clamp) and returns an adjusted top-left, or nothing to leave it
unchanged. Use it for snapping, alignment, or custom bounds. Move only —
resizing a floating group is unaffected.

holds the bounds of the other floating groups (relative
to the same container), snapshotted at drag start, so the callback can
align the dragged group against its siblings.

Controls tab drag-and-drop reorder animation style.

-

: tabs animate smoothly during drag-and-drop reorder —
  tabs slide apart to reveal the insertion gap, then animate to their
  final positions on drop (Chrome-like behavior).
-

: standard tab reorder behavior without animation.

Defaults to

Controls how tab groups are visually indicated in the tab bar.

-

(default): Chrome-style SVG underline that wraps around the active tab
  with rounded corners. Requires JavaScript for positioning and path computation.
-

: Flat continuous colored bar spanning the full tab group width.
  Unlike
, the bar does not curve around the active tab.

A key-value object of anything that is a valid JavaScript Object.

Render pinned tabs icon-only (title + close button hidden), VS-Code /
Chrome style. Default false — dockview's default tab has no favicon, so
pinned tabs stay labelled (with a pin glyph) unless you opt in. Best
enabled alongside a custom tab renderer that shows an icon.
Add a Pin/Unpin item to the tab context menu (requires ContextMenuModule). Default true.
Master switch. Default: undefined (dormant — pinning is a no-op).
(default) keeps pinned tabs first within the existing strip;

renders them on their own VS-Code-style row.
(Phase 1 implements

mode, keep pinned tabs frozen to the left edge when the tab
strip scrolls horizontally (Chrome-style sticky columns); each pinned tab
sits at the cumulative width of the pinned tabs before it. Default true;
set
to let pinned tabs scroll away with the rest. Ignored in
mode, where the pinned row is always visible.

Drag a tab across the pin boundary to toggle its pinned state
(VS-Code-style). Default false — dragging across the boundary is clamped
back, matching Chrome, where pinning is an explicit action only.

; consumers that route edge cells differently can read this.

Display hint: this cell docks as a dedicated edge group (not a grid-edge
split). Purely advisory — a co-installed resolver's UI (e.g. the drop-guide
compass) can suppress its own overlay for these cells so they don't
double-render with the edge-group affordance.

Component rendered as the drag ghost (the small floating chip) while
a group of panels is being dragged. If omitted, the default

ghost is used.

Note: the ghost is captured by the browser at drag start, so the
component must render fast / synchronously enough to be visible
before the browser snapshots it.
